# kysigned — self-verifying e-signatures for agents and humans

> This is a kysigned instance. kysigned is open-source, operator-forkable software: this
> deployment is run by its own operator, on its own domain, with its own branding, pricing,
> and allowlist. The URLs below are relative to this instance's origin.
>
> How it works: /how-it-works (technical: /how-it-works-technical.html)
> Verify a bundle: /verify (offline, no account, no server lookup)
> FAQ: /faq
> Health: /v1/health
> Pricing / access: operator-configured — this instance may charge per envelope, gate creators by
>   an allowlist, or both. Signing is always free for signers.
> Open source: Apache-2.0-licensed (https://github.com/kychee-com/kysigned); run your own with `run402 up`.

**IMPORTANT: kysigned is NOT in the trust chain.** The proof of a signature is the signer's own
email provider, not kysigned. The instance assembles and time-stamps the evidence; anyone can verify
it with math and public keys — even if this instance no longer exists. No accounts for signers, no
certificate authority, no central registry, no lookups.

## Hello, Agent!

kysigned turns a PDF plus a list of signers into a legally-binding, independently-verifiable
e-signature — and it's built for you to drive end to end, no human UI required. You act as the
CREATOR: you create an envelope and track it. Each human signer signs by replying to one email.
When everyone has signed, every party receives a sealed EVIDENCE BUNDLE they can verify forever.

## How a signature actually works

1. You create an envelope (a PDF + signers). kysigned prepends a one-page cover (the ESIGN/UETA +
   eIDAS consent to sign electronically, plus the document's SHA-256) and emails each signer their
   own signing request from this instance's signing mailbox (`forward-to-sign@<this-instance>`).
2. A signer signs by FORWARDING that email back with **`I sign this document`** as the first line,
   the PDF still attached. Their provider (Gmail, Outlook, iCloud, …) DKIM-signs that reply — a
   cryptographic signature only that provider can produce for that mailbox. Signers need no account
   and no app.
3. When all signers are in, kysigned assembles the **evidence bundle**: the original document, every
   signed `.eml` reply, RFC-3161 + OpenTimestamps time proofs, and the DKIM public keys. It's a clean
   unsigned PDF (opens with no certificate warning / no red-X) carrying a verify QR, and it's emailed
   to every party (and you).
4. Anyone verifies the bundle offline — math + public DKIM keys — with no server involved.

## TL;DR for an agent

- **Create**: `POST /v1/envelope` with a PDF + signers → kysigned emails the requests.
- **Track**: `GET /v1/envelope/:id` → per-signer status + signed times. Each non-creator signature
  also emails you a "X just signed Y" progress note.
- **Nudge / cancel**: `POST /v1/envelope/:id/remind`, `POST /v1/envelope/:id/void`.
- **Complete**: everyone receives the bundle automatically. Verify any time at `/verify`.
- **Pay / access**: operator-configured — the creator account may need credits and/or allowlisting.

## Authentication

The creator API (`/v1/envelope*`, `/v1/documents`, `/v1/envelopes`) is authenticated as the CREATOR
account, in one of two ways:

- **API key (the agent path).** Mint a key in the dashboard at `/account/api-keys` (shown exactly
  once, format `ksk_…`) and send it in the `Authorization` header — `Bearer ksk_…` or the bare key
  both work. Keys are creator-scoped (envelope actions only — they cannot manage keys or the
  account), CSRF-exempt, and revocable. Auth failures return `401 { "code": "auth_invalid_key" }`;
  using a key outside its scope returns `403 { "code": "auth_key_scope" }`.
- **Browser session (the human path).** `POST /v1/auth/magic-link` with `{ "email": "you@example.com" }`
  → this instance emails a one-time link; exchange it via `POST /v1/auth/token` for an HttpOnly
  session cookie. (Passkeys are also supported.) State-changing requests on the cookie path require
  the `X-Kysigned-Csrf` header.
- MCP / CLI: set `KYSIGNED_ENDPOINT` (this instance's origin) and `KYSIGNED_AUTHORIZATION` (your
  `ksk_…` API key). See "Agent tools (MCP)" below.
- Signer endpoints (`/v1/sign/:id/:token/info`, `/v1/envelope/:id/:token/pdf`) use the per-signer
  token from the signing-request email — no account needed.
- **Wallet payment (x402) — no account at all.** Where the operator has enabled it (kysigned.com
  has), `POST /v1/x402/envelope` needs NO key and NO session: the payment is the authorization. See
  "Machine payment (x402)" below.

## Machine payment (x402)

Operator-dependent: enabled on kysigned.com; a fork without x402 config answers
`404 { "code": "payment_x402_not_enabled" }` on this route.

An agent holding a wallet can create an envelope with no account, no card, and no API key by paying
the flat per-envelope price per call:

1. `POST /v1/x402/envelope` with the standard create body PLUS `creator_email` (required — the
   deliverable address the creator record, creation/completion mail, and the evidence bundle attach
   to). An unpaid call is answered by the platform with a standard **x402 402 challenge** (payment
   requirements: the per-envelope price in USD micros, the operator's payout wallet, mainnet
   networks) — the x402 protocol shape, not this API's `{error, code}` envelope.
2. Pay with any x402-capable client (sign the payment authorization per the challenge) and retry
   with the `X-PAYMENT` header. The platform verifies and settles BEFORE the endpoint runs.
3. The settled payment credits the creator ledger EXACTLY ONCE (keyed on the stable payment id) and
   the create debits it → `201` with the same response shape as `POST /v1/envelope`. Retrying with
   the same settled payment replays the SAME 201 — one payment, one envelope, no double-charge.

Optional retry control: send your own `Idempotency-Key` header on the paid call — a retry with the
same key replays the same envelope (the create dedupes on your key); without one, dedupe keys on the
settled payment id. The ledger credit is always exactly-once per settled payment either way.

**Validate before you pay (avoid a wasted charge).** run402 settles the payment BEFORE this endpoint
runs, so a paid create that then fails deterministic validation (a plus-alias signer, a bad email, an
unparseable/oversize PDF) still consumes the charge. Call `POST /v1/envelope/preflight` FIRST — it's
free (no charge, no create) and runs the same input validation, returning `{ "ok": true }` or the
same `validation_*` / `rate_size_*` error. Pay only once preflight passes. If you skip preflight and a
paid create fails validation, the response carries `payment_banked: true`, `credit_email`, and
`next_actions`: the money became recoverable account credit for `creator_email` (NOT lost) — fix the
input, sign in to `creator_email` (magic link), and create via the authenticated `POST /v1/envelope`;
the banked credit covers it, no new payment.

**Payment receipt.** A successful `201` includes a `payment` block — `payment_id`, `network`,
`amount_usd_micros`, `asset`, `pay_to`, `settlement_reference` (tx hash), `settled_at` — so you can
reconcile the spend with the envelope. A `tracking` block notes that `status_url` needs creator auth.

**Insufficient funds.** If a paid retry returns `402 { "code": "payment_insufficient_funds", ... }`,
your wallet lacks enough USDC on Base mainnet for the quoted price. Its `next_actions` carries
`{ "type": "fund_wallet", "network", "asset", "minimum_amount_usd_micros" }` — fund the wallet
accordingly and retry. (Emitted by the run402 gateway before this endpoint runs; the raw facilitator
reason and the required vs available amounts are under `details`.)

Discovery: an UNFUNDED `POST /v1/envelope` returns
`402 { "code": "payment_required", "x402_route": "/v1/x402/envelope", "x402_price_usd_micros": <price> }`
so you can fall over to the paid route programmatically. Dashboard continuity: sign in later with
the `creator_email` you supplied (magic link / passkey) and the envelope + its wallet-sourced ledger
entries are in the dashboard like any creator's. The x402 route is plain HTTP for any x402 client —
and `kysigned-mcp` drives it first-class: its `wallet_status` + `create_envelope_x402` tools pay
from the host-local run402 wallet with NO API key (see "Agent tools (MCP)"); the five ops tools
stay key-authenticated.

## Machine-readable errors

Every `/v1` error response is `{ "error": "<human message>", "code": "<stable code>" }`. Codes are a
fixed taxonomy mapped to the status contract: `auth_*` (401/403), `csrf_*` (403), `payment_*`
(the credit-gate `payment_required` 402; the x402 route's `payment_x402_not_enabled` 404 /
`payment_x402_mismatch` 409 / `payment_x402_unavailable` 503), `validation_*` (400), `state_*`
(409 lifecycle conflicts, e.g. editing a sealed envelope),
`not_found` (404), `rate_size_*` (413/400 size guards), `idempotency_*` (Idempotency-Key semantics),
`internal_*` (5xx). Branch on `code`, display `error`.

## Reliability (idempotency + webhooks)

- **Idempotent create.** Send an `Idempotency-Key` header (≤ 200 chars) on `POST /v1/envelope`: a
  retried create with the same key returns the SAME envelope with exactly one charge; the same key
  with a different payload returns `409 { "code": "idempotency_key_reuse" }`. Always set it when you
  retry on timeouts.
- **Signed completion webhooks.** Pass `callback_url` (https only) at create; the 201 response
  returns `callback_secret` (`whs_…`) EXACTLY ONCE. When the envelope completes, this instance POSTs
  `{ "type": "envelope.completed", "envelope_id", "document_name", "status", "completed_at",
  "signers": [...] }` to your URL with the header
  `X-Kysigned-Signature: t=<unix seconds>,v1=<hex hmac-sha256(callback_secret, "<t>." + rawBody)>`.
  Verify by recomputing the HMAC over `t + "." + rawBody`, comparing constant-time, and rejecting
  timestamps outside your tolerance (e.g. 5 minutes). Deliveries retry on failure (at-least-once) —
  make your receiver idempotent on `envelope_id`.

The full surface is also machine-readable as OpenAPI 3.1 at `/openapi.json`.

## The agent journey (creator)

1. Ensure your creator account can create envelopes on this instance (credits and/or allowlist, per
   the operator's configuration).
2. Create the envelope:

   ```
   POST /v1/envelope
   Authorization: Bearer ksk_<your key>
   Idempotency-Key: <your retry-safe key>
   {
     "document_name": "Mutual NDA",
     "pdf_base64": "<base64-encoded PDF>",
     "signers": [ { "email": "jane@acme.com", "name": "Jane Roe" } ],
     "message": "Please review and sign our mutual NDA.",
     "callback_url": "https://your-agent.example.com/kysigned-hook"
   }
   ```
   → `{ "envelope_id", "status", "document_hash", "status_url", "verify_url", "signing_links": [...], "callback_secret"?, "tracking" }`
   (`callback_secret` appears only when you sent `callback_url`, and only this once — store it to
   verify webhook deliveries.)
   `tracking.token` (`ktt_…`) is the envelope-observer handle, returned on EVERY create: send it as
   the `Authorization` header on `GET /v1/envelope/:id` to poll status — full signer roster with
   per-signer signing + delivery state — with no account, API key, or session. It is read-only and
   envelope-scoped: any other envelope answers 404, and every mutating/listing route refuses it
   (`403 auth_tracking_scope`). Hand it to a CI job or a third party to share tracking WITHOUT
   sharing your API key. Lost it after a restart? The free `POST /v1/envelope/preflight` replay
   (`creator_email` + your `idempotency_key`) returns the same create body, token included.
   Each `signing_links[]` entry is `{ email, name, link, review_link }`. `review_link`
   (`/review/:id/:token`) is the human, browser-openable review page you can hand a signer directly.
   `link` (`/v1/sign/:id/:token`) is the programmatic signer-token API base (`GET .../info` plus the
   POST-to-sign call); it is not meant to be opened in a browser. Normally signers receive the invite
   email and use neither.

   Notes: ≤ 20 signers; each PDF must be ≤ ~3 MB when sent as `pdf_base64` (the create call is invoked
   synchronously on Lambda, whose event payload is capped at 6 MiB; base64-in-JSON plus the gateway's own
   wrapping inflates a raw PDF by ~16/9, so ~3 MB is the safe ceiling — larger bodies fail at the gateway).
   To send a larger document, pass `pdf_url` instead of `pdf_base64` (the bytes are fetched server-side,
   bypassing the request-body limit). Separately, oversize envelopes are rejected up front with the size
   math: the completed bundle re-attaches each signer's PDF, so large documents × many signers can exceed
   the 15 MiB bundle limit even when each upload fits.

3. Track: `GET /v1/envelope/:id` until every signer is `signed`.
4. Optional: `POST /v1/envelope/:id/remind` to nudge pending signers; `POST /v1/envelope/:id/void` to
   cancel. Until the envelope is sealed you can edit recipients with `POST` / `PATCH` / `DELETE`
   `/v1/envelope/:id/signers` (the signer's address rides in `?email=`).
5. On completion every party receives the evidence-bundle email, and — if you set `callback_url` —
   your receiver gets the signed `envelope.completed` webhook. The bundle is the durable record —
   verify it any time at `/verify`. (No server-side verify endpoint exists; verification is client-side.)

## Agent tools (MCP)

`npx kysigned-mcp` exposes seven tools. The five ops tools are key-authenticated — configure
`KYSIGNED_ENDPOINT` + `KYSIGNED_AUTHORIZATION`:

- **create_envelope** — `POST /v1/envelope` (`document_name`, `pdf_base64` | `pdf_url`, `signers[]`, `message?`)
- **check_envelope_status** — `GET /v1/envelope/:id`; also accepts `tracking_token` (`ktt_…` from any
  create result) instead of the key — the no-key observer path, envelope-scoped and read-only
- **list_envelopes** — `GET /v1/envelopes` (the creator's own envelopes)
- **send_reminder** — `POST /v1/envelope/:id/remind`
- **void_envelope** — `POST /v1/envelope/:id/void`

The wallet pair needs NO key — it pays from the host-local run402 allowance wallet (`run402 init`;
the private key is never a tool argument and never appears in output):

- **wallet_status** — read-only payment readiness: address, network, asset, balance, the live
  per-envelope price (read from the x402 route's own challenge), funding guidance when short.
- **create_envelope_x402** — the wallet-paid create (annotated destructive so hosts confirm the
  spend): runs the free preflight FIRST, then a balance gate — an invalid request or short balance
  never charges — then pays via x402 and returns the envelope + `payment` receipt + `tracking`
  note. Retries are safe: pass `idempotency_key` (or reuse the returned `spending_intent_key`) —
  the preflight's intent lookup answers `already_created` and the tool returns the stored envelope
  with `replayed: true`, paying nothing (the route is always-priced, so a blind re-send would
  settle a second payment).

Verification is intentionally NOT a tool — the bundle verifies client-side at `/verify` or with the
reference verifier in the public repo.

## API reference (public surface)

| Method & path | Auth | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `GET /v1/health` | none | Liveness check → `{ status, service, ts }`. |
| `POST /v1/auth/magic-link` | none | Email the creator a one-time sign-in link. |
| `POST /v1/auth/token` | none | Exchange a magic-link (or passkey) for a creator session. |
| `POST /v1/envelope` | creator | Create an envelope from a PDF + signers; emails each request. |
| `POST /v1/x402/envelope` | x402 payment | Machine-paid create: pay per envelope with a wallet, no account (operator-dependent). |
| `GET /v1/envelope/:id` | creator | Envelope status + per-signer state and signed times. |
| `POST /v1/envelope/:id/remind` | creator | Remind all pending signers. |
| `POST /v1/envelope/:id/void` | creator | Cancel an active envelope and notify signers. |
| `POST /v1/envelope/:id/signers` | creator | Add a recipient (allowed until the envelope is sealed). |
| `PATCH /v1/envelope/:id/signers` | creator | Edit a recipient (`?email=`), until sealed. |
| `DELETE /v1/envelope/:id/signers` | creator | Remove a recipient (`?email=`), until sealed. |
| `POST /v1/envelope/:id/seal` | creator | Manually seal + distribute once everyone has signed. |
| `GET /v1/envelope/:id/pdf` | creator | Download the creator's copy of the document. |
| `GET /v1/envelopes` | creator | List the creator's envelopes (id, name, status, dates). |
| `GET /v1/documents` | creator | List the creator's uploaded documents (grouped by hash). |
| `POST /v1/api-keys` | session only | Mint a creator API key (`ksk_…`, returned exactly once). |
| `GET /v1/api-keys` | session only | List your API keys (metadata only, never the value). |
| `DELETE /v1/api-keys/:id` | session only | Revoke a key (effective on its next use). |
| `GET /v1/sign/:id/:token/info` | signer token | Signer review-page data (name, document, hash). |
| `GET /v1/envelope/:id/:token/pdf` | signer token | Signer downloads their copy of the document. |

"creator" auth = an API key in `Authorization` OR the browser session. Key management is
session-only by design: a leaked key can never mint more keys.

Not part of the public agent surface: the operator allowlist admin, the inbound-mail webhook, and any
operator-specific credit/billing routes. There is **no** server-side verify endpoint — verification is
fully client-side (the `/verify` page or the CLI).

## Verifying a bundle

The bundle is an ordinary PDF anyone can verify with no account and no server:

- **Browser**: open `/verify` and drop the bundle in. It re-computes the document hash, checks each
  signer's DKIM signature against the embedded and publicly-archived keys, validates the RFC-3161 and
  OpenTimestamps time proofs, and confirms each signer signed THIS document.
- **CLI**: a reference verifier ships in the public repository.

The verdict is one of four honest assurance tiers, not a yes/no. Offline, anyone reaches INTEGRITY
VERIFIED (valid signatures, matching document, intent line, timestamp present). Going online confirms
the exact provider key against the public archive (PROVIDER KEY CONFIRMED) and a Bitcoin-anchored,
un-back-datable time within the key's recorded lifetime window (PROVEN DURABLE). A key an operator merely
claimed was the provider's fails the archive gate instead of passing as proven.

## Fork your own

kysigned is open source and operator-forkable: run your own instance on your own domain, with your
own branding, pay-per-envelope or allowlist-gated. The Apache-2.0-licensed public repository (with the
README quickstart) shows how; point agents at your instance via the MCP's `KYSIGNED_ENDPOINT`.

## Human FAQ

Your agent may share this with you.

- **What am I getting?** A legally-binding e-signature (ESIGN/UETA in the US, eIDAS SES/AES in the EU)
  whose proof lives in your and your signers' inboxes — verifiable forever, by anyone, without trusting
  this instance.
- **Do signers need an account?** No. They sign by forwarding one email — no app, no login.
- **What does it cost?** Operator-configured. Signing is always free for signers.
- **What if this instance disappears?** Your evidence bundle still verifies — it's math and your
  providers' public keys, not a lookup against any server.
